title: Unless The Lord Does It
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Unless The Lord Does It __ Psalm 127 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Introduction: Psalm 127 — a Psalm of Solomon
Unless the Lord builds the house, the builders labor in vain
Unless the Lord guards the city, the guards watch in vain
Rising early and working late — God gives to His beloved in sleep
Children as a heritage from the Lord
Sons like arrows in the hand of a warrior
Conclusion: All of life's work rests on God's blessing
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Psalm 127, Solomon's declaration that all human effort apart from God is in vain. Whether building a house, guarding a city, or raising a family, the blessing of the Lord is essential — children are a heritage from Him, and all fruitful labor depends on His sovereign hand.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Psalm for Covenant Baptism

For the second week in a row, we are going to be having a covenant baptism today. Psalm 127 is a fitting Psalm for the occasion of a covenant baptism — the baptism of a child born into a Christian home, where one or both parents are believers in the Lord Jesus Christ.

If you look at the Psalm, even in the way it is laid out in your Bible, you can see that it divides into two portions: verses 1 and 2, and then verses 3 through 5. But there is an underlying theme that unites both parts. Whether you are building a house, protecting a city, or raising a family — unless the Lord is doing it through you, you are wasting your time. **Dependence on the Lord, and the futility of life without him, are the main message of this Psalm.**

I want to do three things as we work through it: first, consider some background about the Psalm; second, see that we must depend on the Lord at all times; and third, apply that dependence to the raising of children.

## Background: A Song of Ascents by Solomon

If you look at the title before verse 1, you will see that this is "a song of ascents" — these are the songs the pilgrims sang as they approached Jerusalem during the three great pilgrimage feasts of the Old Testament. No matter where you were in the world, if you were going to Jerusalem, you were going *up* — because Jerusalem sits on Mount Zion, the holy hill of the Lord. As the pilgrims ascended, they would sing these songs to the Lord, and Psalm 127 is one of them.

The Psalm is also attributed to Solomon, one of only two Psalms by him. Solomon was uniquely qualified to speak about building, protecting, and having children without the Lord. For most of his adult life, Solomon was not following the Lord — he was chasing after the world around him. Later in life he returned and saw the futility of that path, which is why he wrote Ecclesiastes — and perhaps this Psalm as well. He was an expert at doing these three great things without the Lord, and he saw how poorly it went.

## All of Life Must Be Lived in Dependence on the Lord

Derek Kidner writes about this Psalm: "One of the most telling features of this short poem is that it singles out three of our most universal preoccupations — building, security, raising a family. It makes us ask what they all amount to, and to whom we owe them."

### Building a House

> *"Unless the Lord builds the house, they labor in vain who build it."* (Psalm 127:1a)

The "house" here could be a literal construction or the building of a family household. Solomon tells us by the Spirit's inspiration: unless the Lord is building that house through you, unless your family is God-centered, all the good things and hard work you do will amount to nothing of lasting worth.

A God-centered home is one in which Christ reigns over every area of its life — over the family's faith, its struggles, and yes, its conflicts. You know that Christian families fight, right? It would be foolish to think otherwise. When you put sinners together — people who are by nature selfish — there will be conflicts. The difference between a Christian family and other families is that its members are committed to working through those struggles according to what the Lord Jesus Christ says.

### Guarding a City

> *"Unless the Lord guards the city, the watchman stays awake in vain."* (Psalm 127:1b)

We can put all kinds of protections around our families, and we should. But we tend to begin trusting those protections rather than the Lord who gives them. We trust our parenting skills, our prayers, our children's education, the safeguards we have put in place, our sound theology — and we forget that it is the Lord who does the keeping.

Consider the story of the Great Wall of China. The Chinese built it so high no one could climb over it, so thick nothing could break it down. Yet in the first hundred years of its existence, China was invaded three times. Not once did the invaders break through or climb over. Each time, they bribed a gatekeeper and walked in through the gates. The lesson: the Chinese were so busy relying on walls of stone that they forgot to build integrity into their people. Christian parents know better than that. We do not trust in the things the Lord has given us to protect our children. We trust in the Lord himself, who is the guardian of his people.

> *"My help comes from the Lord, who made heaven and earth. He will not allow your foot to be moved; he who keeps you will not slumber."* (Psalm 121:2–3)

It is the Lord who keeps us — not we ourselves. If the Lord stopped keeping us for even a nanosecond, we would abandon him. It is because of his faithfulness to us that we stay close to him.

### Working Hard to Provide

> *"It is vain for you to rise up early, to sit up late, to eat the bread of sorrows; for so he gives his beloved sleep."* (Psalm 127:2)

The picture here is of a person working hard without the Lord — up early, up late, and all he has to show for it is the bread of sorrow. This is the figure described in Ecclesiastes: someone who has done everything under the sun apart from faith in God and found no satisfaction in any of it. The work without the Lord still produces *something* — there is bread — but it goes nowhere. Life without Christ becomes a rat race. You have never seen a U-Haul behind a hearse, as the saying goes.

But to those who belong to him, God gives a purpose to their work and to their lives that transcends the daily grind: **his glory and our joy.** And this is signified in the Psalm by the sweet sleep given to those whose life is centered on Christ, while those going on their own are anxious about tomorrow.

Think of Jesus in the boat during the storm in Mark 4. The disciples are terrified; water is filling the boat. And Jesus? He was asleep in the stern on a cushion — the beloved Son of God, at rest, because he knew his Father was in control. That is what happens when we are living a life that glorifies God: we rest in the confidence that our Father in heaven is in control, even in the midst of the storm. Life often feels like a storm. Parenting often feels like a storm. But even in the midst of it, God gives his beloved sleep.

**How do we live dependently on the Lord in all of this?**

First, **listen to Jesus**. You do not need to reinvent the wheel. At the end of the Sermon on the Mount:

> *"Therefore whoever hears these sayings of mine and does them, I will liken him to a wise man who built his house on the rock."* (Matthew 7:24)

And the climax of the Sermon on the Mount is in Matthew 6:33:

> *"Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you."* (Matthew 6:33)

Whatever we are doing — building a home, raising children — we ask: how is the kingdom of God being manifested in what I am doing? Is what I am doing Christlike? Am I decreasing so that Christ may increase? Would it make a difference in what I am doing, and why, if I had never met Christ?

## Dependence on the Lord Applied to Raising Children

> *"Behold, children are a heritage from the Lord, the fruit of the womb is a reward. Like arrows in the hand of a warrior, so are the children of one's youth. Happy is the man who has his quiver full of them; they shall not be ashamed, but shall speak with their enemies in the gate."* (Psalm 127:3–5)

### Children Are a Gift

In Jewish practice, this song was recited as part of a thanksgiving service after the birth of a child. Children of God's people are an inheritance from the Lord, a gift from him. And if they are a gift from the Lord, they belong to him — because God can only give what is his.

Charles Hodge, on the occasion of his first daughter's baptism in December 1825, wrote to his mother: "I never so highly appreciated before the privilege of giving to God what is dearest to us on earth... We feel now as though she were not our own, but something lent to us, to be cultivated and prepared through our agency for heaven." Brothers and sisters, that is what we are doing with our children. Our children are on loan from God, and we are preparing them to return to him. That has to be our goal in parenting. It does not matter how far they can throw a ball or how fast they can run. What do they believe? Do they know the Lord Jesus? That is what we are after.

### Children Are Arrows That Must Be Built and Aimed

> *"Like arrows in the hand of a warrior, so are the children of one's youth."* (Psalm 127:4)

Children are a heritage and a reward — and yet they can be a tremendous challenge and a test of patience as well. The Psalmist does not say children are a gift because they are always sweet and easy. Just as the Promised Land — described in similar language as a heritage from the Lord — took years of struggle to possess, so it goes with the bundles of joy entrusted to parents.

For an arrow to work, it must be properly built: the shaft must be straight, the notch correctly placed, the fletching just right so the arrow spins true, the head the proper weight. All of that implies work.

> *"Fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord."* (Ephesians 6:4)

Fathers, God addresses you. You are responsible for your children. The training is what is done *to* and *with* the child — structure, discipline, modeling, instruction in righteousness. The admonition is what is said *to* the child and modeled *before* the child. And the children must be pointed to the right target. They must be set before the Lord continually.

Are we preparing the arrows the Lord has given us for battle? Or are we sacrificing our children on the altar of the world? In Ezekiel 16, God charges Israel: "You took your sons and your daughters whom you bore to me, and you sacrificed them... You have slain my children." What are our educational choices doing with our children? How is the way we live as husbands and wives training our arrows to fly straight? How is our commitment to the church — or lack of it — forming them?

### The Fruit of Faithfully Raised Children

Children raised in the nurturing and admonition of the Lord will be a blessing to their parents. The picture in verse 5 is of a man standing at the city gate — the public square, where business is done and cases are heard — and all anyone can see behind him is his children: "You want to deal with my father? You are dealing with all of us." A quiver full of arrows, ready for the warrior.

Children, take note: your parents took care of you. When they age, it is your duty to take care of them. They changed your diapers; there may come a time when you need to repay that favor. After being raised in the nurturing and admonition of the Lord, care for those who lovingly and sacrificially pointed you to your God.

## Conclusion

All of life must be centered on God in Christ — including, and especially, the raising of our children. Because unless the Lord does it, those who do it do it in vain. May that not be said of us.
